informal_statement.text: Given equal-length slices of non-negative item weights and item values, and a non-negative integer capacity, return the maximum total value achievable by choosing a subset whose total weight does not exceed the capacity. Each item may be selected at most once.
preconditions: weights and values have the same length
every weight is a non-negative integer
every value is a non-negative integer
required_properties: the result is witnessed by some subset S of item indices whose total weight <= capacity and total value equals the result
no subset whose total weight <= capacity has a strictly greater total value
each item index is used at most once in any witness subset
edge_cases: capacity 0 (result is 0 unless there is a zero-weight positive-value item)
a single item with weight > capacity
multiple items of identical weight and value

semantic_units.description: Alignment precondition: weights and values have the same length.
common_failure_modes: allowing trailing values to be ignored

semantic_units.description: Capacity feasibility: the witness subset has total weight at most the capacity.
Value witness: there is a valid selection whose total value equals the returned result.
common_failure_modes: stating only a value upper bound

semantic_units.description: Optimality: no valid selection with total weight <= capacity achieves a strictly greater total value.
0/1 uniqueness: items appear at most once in any witness selection.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ws : Weights) (vs : Values) (cap : Nat), ws.length = vs.length → ∃ (sel : Selection), ValidSelection ws sel ∧ totalWeight ws sel ≤ cap ∧ totalValue vs sel = knapsack01 ws vs cap
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When weights and values are length-aligned, there exists a valid feasible selection whose total value equals the returned result.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ws : Weights) (vs : Values) (cap : Nat) (sel : Selection), ws.length = vs.length → ValidSelection ws sel → totalWeight ws sel ≤ cap → totalValue vs sel ≤ knapsack01 ws vs cap
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When weights and values are length-aligned, no valid feasible selection beats the returned result.

generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The implementation asserts that `weights.len() == values.len()`, so the proof context must assume the lengths match.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
let cap : Nat := capacity.toNat
let dp0 : List Nat := List.replicate (cap+1) 0
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the capacity as a natural number and the initial DP table as an array of length `cap+1` filled with 0.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ c : Nat, c ≤ cap → (dp[c] : Nat) is well-defined during the algorithm
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Show that all DP accesses `dp[c]` and `dp[c-w]` are within bounds whenever they occur (using the loop invariants and the check `w ≤ cap`).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: For each outer iteration index i (0 ≤ i ≤ n), there exists a function best_i : Fin (cap+1) → Nat such that for all c ≤ cap, dp[c] = best_i ⟨c, _⟩, where best_i c equals the maximum total value achievable using items from indices < i with total weight ≤ c.
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: State the main correctness invariant: after processing the first `i` items, `dp[c]` stores the optimal value for capacity `c` using only those items.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
Assuming the invariant holds for i, after processing item i with weight w and value v (and skipping if w > cap), the update for each c ≤ cap satisfies:
`dp_new[c] = max dp_old[c] (dp_old[c - w] + v)` for c ≥ w, and `dp_new[c] = dp_old[c]` for c < w.
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove that the inner reverse loop implements the 0/1 knapsack recurrence for each capacity: either do not take the item (keep old value) or take it (add value to the best for remaining capacity).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: Because the inner loop iterates `c` in decreasing order from `w` to `cap`, for each c ≥ w the expression `dp[c - w]` used to compute `cand` refers to the dp state before updating capacity c (i.e., it corresponds to `dp_old[c - w]`).
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Justify why the 1-D rolling array is correct: iterating capacities in reverse prevents using the same item multiple times.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: If w > cap then for all c ≤ cap, dp_new[c] = dp_old[c].
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: When an item’s weight exceeds the capacity, the code continues without changing the DP table; prove the DP remains unchanged.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: For i = 0, for all c ≤ cap, dp[c] = 0, which equals the maximum value achievable using no items with total weight ≤ c.
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Show the base case: with zero items, the best value is 0 for any capacity.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: Let n := weights.length. After the outer loop completes, for all c ≤ cap, dp[c] equals the maximum total value achievable using items with indices < n and total weight ≤ c.
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Conclude the invariant at i = n: the DP table represents the optimal values for all capacities after considering all items.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: dp[cap] = maxValue (subsets of {0..n-1} with totalWeight ≤ cap) (sum of corresponding values), and the function returns dp[cap].
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The function returns `dp[cap]`, so prove that this equals the maximum total value over all subsets whose total weight is at most `capacity`.
